subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>≠</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are two common tangents of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>≠</mo><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are two common tangents of circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Equation of the tangent to the parabola can be written as y=mx+14m or y-mx-14m=0

Now, the perpendicular distance from 0,0 to the tangent is equal to the radius of the given circle, soOM=2⇒-14m1+m2=2

⇒216m21+m2=1  ⇒32m4+32m2-1=0

⇒  m2=-25+2316+22×25 ignoring the negative sign as it is square function.

Now 8m1m2=8m2

=-25+2316+223=32-4

subject: Mathematics
q: Let A be the focus of the parabola $y^{2}=8 x$. Let the line $y=\mathrm{m} x+\mathrm{c}$ intersect the parabola at two distinct points $B$ and $C$. If the centroid of the triangle $A B C$ is $\left(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$, then $(B C)^{2}$ is equal to :
question: Let A be the focus of the parabola $y^{2}=8 x$. Let the line $y=\mathrm{m} x+\mathrm{c}$ intersect the parabola at two distinct points $B$ and $C$. If the centroid of the triangle $A B C$ is $\left(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$, then $(B C)^{2}$ is equal to :
solution:
Coordinates of centroid of triangle ABC are
$\dfrac{2}{3}(t_1^2 + t_2^2 + 1) = \dfrac{7}{3} \Rightarrow t_1^2 + t_2^2 = \dfrac{5}{2}$
$\dfrac{4}{3}(t_1 + t_2) = \dfrac{4}{3} \Rightarrow t_1 + t_2 = 1$
$(t_1 + t_2)^2 = t_1^2 + t_2^2 + 2t_1 t_2 \Rightarrow t_1 t_2 = \dfrac{-3}{4}$
$(t_1 - t_2)^2 = (t_1 + t_2)^2 - 4t_1 t_2 = 4$
$(BC)^2 = 4(t_1^2 - t_2^2)^2 + 16(t_1 - t_2)^2$
$\Rightarrow (BC)^2 = 80$

subject: Mathematics
q: A circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> unit passes through the vertex and the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and touches the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______.
question: A circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> unit passes through the vertex and the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and touches the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
The vertex and focus of parabola y2=2x are V0,0 and S12,0 respectively

Let the equation of circle passing through vertex and focus of the parabola be x-h2+y-k2=4

∵ Circle passes through 0,0

⇒h2+k2=4     ⋯1

∵ Circle passes through 12,0

12-h2+k2=4

⇒h2+k2=h+154     ⋯2

On solving 1 and 2

4=h+154

⇒h=14

⇒k=634

here k=-634 is rejected as circle with centre 14,-634 can't touch the parabola y2=2x.

So the equation of circle will be x-142+k-6342=4

Since this circle touches the parabola y=x-142+α

i.e. α=2+634=8+634

4α-8=63

4α-82=63

subject: Mathematics
q: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $y^2=4x$ and its chords OP and OQ are perpendicular to each other. If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment PQ is a conic C, then the length of its latus rectum is:
question: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $y^2=4x$ and its chords OP and OQ are perpendicular to each other. If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment PQ is a conic C, then the length of its latus rectum is:
solution:
Let the vertex of the parabola $y^2 = 4x$ be $O(0, 0)$.

Let the coordinates of the points $P$ and $Q$ on the parabola be $(t_1^2, 2t_1)$ and $(t_2^2, 2t_2)$ respectively.

The slope of the chord $OP$ is $m_1 = \dfrac{2t_1 - 0}{t_1^2 - 0} = \dfrac{2}{t_1}$.

The slope of the chord $OQ$ is $m_2 = \dfrac{2t_2 - 0}{t_2^2 - 0} = \dfrac{2}{t_2}$.

Since $OP$ and $OQ$ are perpendicular to each other, $m_1 m_2 = -1$.

$\Rightarrow \left(\dfrac{2}{t_1}\right) \left(\dfrac{2}{t_2}\right) = -1$

$\Rightarrow t_1 t_2 = -4$

Let $M(h, k)$ be the mid-point of the line segment $PQ$. Then,

$h = \dfrac{t_1^2 + t_2^2}{2} \Rightarrow t_1^2 + t_2^2 = 2h$

$k = \dfrac{2t_1 + 2t_2}{2} \Rightarrow t_1 + t_2 = k$

Squaring the equation for $k$, we get:

$k^2 = (t_1 + t_2)^2 = t_1^2 + t_2^2 + 2t_1 t_2$

Substituting the values of $t_1^2 + t_2^2$ and $t_1 t_2$, we obtain:

$k^2 = 2h + 2(-4)$

$k^2 = 2h - 8$

$k^2 = 2(h - 4)$

Replacing $h$ with $x$ and $k$ with $y$, the locus of the mid-point is:

$y^2 = 2(x - 4)$

This represents a parabola of the form $Y^2 = 4AX$, where the length of the latus rectum is $4A = 2$.

Answer: $2$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be the locus of the mirror image of a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> with respect to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. Then the equation of tangent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be the locus of the mirror image of a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> with respect to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. Then the equation of tangent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
Given y2=4x

Mirror image on y=x⇒C : x2=4y

2x=4·dydx⇒dydx=x2

dydxP2,1=22=1

Equation of tangent at 2,1

⇒y-1=1×x-2

⇒x-y=1

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement 1: $y=m x-\frac{1}{m}$ is always a tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-4 x$ for all non-zero values of $m$.
Statement 2: Every tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-4 x$ will meet its axis at a point whose abscissa is non-negative.
question:
Statement 1: $y=m x-\frac{1}{m}$ is always a tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-4 x$ for all non-zero values of $m$.
Statement 2: Every tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-4 x$ will meet its axis at a point whose abscissa is non-negative.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true; Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution: Both the given statements are true. Statement $-2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $-1$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the tangent to the conic, <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>)</mo></math> touches the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi></math> (for some fixed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>) at a point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>;</mo></math> then <math><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is
question: If the tangent to the conic, <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>)</mo></math> touches the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi></math> (for some fixed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>) at a point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>;</mo></math> then <math><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is
solution:
The equation of tangent (T=0) would be 12 y+10- 6=2x

4x-y+2=0



The center of the circle is (-4,1) and the point of contact would be the foot of perpendicular from (-4,1) on 4x-y+2=0

α+44=β-1-1= --16-1+242+12

α+44=1517 and β-1-1=1517

α= -817, β= -1517+1=217

Hence option -817, 217 is correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let PQ be a focal chord of the parabola y2=36x of length 100, making an acute angle with the positive x-axis. Let the ordinate of P be positive and M be the point on the line segment PQ such that PM:MQ=3:1. Then which of the following points does NOT lie on the line passing through M and perpendicular to the line PQ?
question: Let PQ be a focal chord of the parabola y2=36x of length 100, making an acute angle with the positive x-axis. Let the ordinate of P be positive and M be the point on the line segment PQ such that PM:MQ=3:1. Then which of the following points does NOT lie on the line passing through M and perpendicular to the line PQ?
solution:
We have been given the length of focal chord PQ=100.

 

Length of focal chord at t=at+1t2=100

Where a=9

t+1t=±103

∴t=3,13,-3,-13

Since ordinate of P is +ve

∴t=3 or 13

(t=3 as slope of PQ is positive)

∴M(21,9)

Required line:y-9=-8060(x-21)

⇒4x+3y=111

-3,43 does not lie on line.

Hence this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $P$ be the point $(1,0)$ and $Q$ a point on the locus $y^2=8 x$. The locus of mid point of $P Q$ is
question: Let $P$ be the point $(1,0)$ and $Q$ a point on the locus $y^2=8 x$. The locus of mid point of $P Q$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{P}=(1,0)$
$Q=(h, k)$ such that $k^2=8 h$
Let $(\alpha, \beta)$ be the midpoint of PQ
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{h}+1}{2}, \quad \beta=\frac{\mathrm{k}+0}{2} \\
& 2 \alpha-1=\mathrm{h}, \quad 2 \beta=\mathrm{k} . \\
& (2 \beta)^2=8(2 \alpha-1) \Rightarrow \beta^2=4 \alpha-2 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}^2-4 \mathrm{x}+2=0 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let A and B be the two points of intersection of the line $y+5=0$ and the mirror image of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ with respect to the line $x+y+4=0$. If d denotes the distance between A and B , and a denotes the area of $\triangle S A B$, where $S$ is the focus of the parabola $y^2=4 x$, then the value of $(a+d)$ is $\qquad$ -
question: Let A and B be the two points of intersection of the line $y+5=0$ and the mirror image of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ with respect to the line $x+y+4=0$. If d denotes the distance between A and B , and a denotes the area of $\triangle S A B$, where $S$ is the focus of the parabola $y^2=4 x$, then the value of $(a+d)$ is $\qquad$ -
solution: To find image of $P\left(t^2, 2 t\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-t^2}{1}=\frac{y-2 t}{1}=\frac{-2\left(t^2+2 t+4\right)}{1^2+1^2}=-(t+1)^2-3 \\ & x=t^2-(t+1)^2-3=-2 t-4 \\ & y=2 t-(t+1)^2-3=-t^2-4 \\ & t=\frac{-x-4}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow y+4=-\left(\frac{-x-4}{2}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(y+4)=-\frac{(x+4)^2}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=-4 y \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Focus }(-4,-5) \end{aligned}$ $\text { Also, } y=-5 \text { intersect }$ $\begin{aligned} & \therefore(-4)(-1)=(x+4)^2 \\ & 4=(x+4)^2 \\ & x+4= \pm 2 \\ & x=-2,-6 \\ & \Rightarrow d=4 \\ & a=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -5 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|[1(-5+5)+1(10-30)]| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(20) \\ & a=10 \\ & \therefore \quad a+d=14\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $x^{2}=4 y$ and Q be any point on it. Let the locus of the point P, which divides the line segment OQ internally in the ratio $2: 3$ be the conic C. Then the equation of the chord of $C$, which is bisected at the point $(1,2)$, is :
question: Let O be the vertex of the parabola $x^{2}=4 y$ and Q be any point on it. Let the locus of the point P, which divides the line segment OQ internally in the ratio $2: 3$ be the conic C. Then the equation of the chord of $C$, which is bisected at the point $(1,2)$, is :
solution:
Parabola $x^2 = 4y$, vertex O = (0, 0), Q = $(2t, t^2)$ on parabola.
P divides OQ in ratio 2:3: $P = \left(\frac{4t}{5}, \frac{2t^2}{5}\right)$.
Eliminating $t$: $x^2 = \frac{8y}{5}$ (conic C with $a = \frac{2}{5}$).
For chord with midpoint $(h, k)$: $hx - 2ay = h^2 - 2ak$.
At $(1, 2)$: $x - \frac{4y}{5} = 1 - \frac{8}{5} = -\frac{3}{5}$.
$5x - 4y + 3 = 0$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the triangle whose one vertex is at the vertex of the parabola, $y^{2}+4\left(x-a^{2}\right)=0$ and the other two vertices are the points of intersection of the parabola and $y$ -axis, is 250 sq. units, then a value of 'a' is :
question: If the area of the triangle whose one vertex is at the vertex of the parabola, $y^{2}+4\left(x-a^{2}\right)=0$ and the other two vertices are the points of intersection of the parabola and $y$ -axis, is 250 sq. units, then a value of 'a' is :
solution:
$y^{t}=-4\left(x-a^{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{Area}=\frac{1}{2}(4 a)\left(a^{2}\right)=2 a^{3}$ Since $2 a^{3}=250 \Rightarrow a=5$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> be a parabola with focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>.Let the tangents to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> with the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> touch the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are collinear is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> be a parabola with focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>.Let the tangents to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> with the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> touch the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are collinear is:
solution:
Lines making angle π4 with y=3x+5 

So using formula tanα=m2-m11+m1m2, we have slope -2 & 12.

Which are perpendicular to each-other so, A, S, Bare collinear for all a>0. (by property that perpendicular tangents will lie on directrix and chord of contact will pass through focus, so the points are collinear )

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the point $P$ be the vertex of the parabola $y = x^2 - 6x + 12$. If a line passing through the point $P$ intersects the circle $x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y + 3 = 0$ at the points $R$ and $S$, then the maximum value of $(PR + PS)^2$ is :
question: Let the point $P$ be the vertex of the parabola $y = x^2 - 6x + 12$. If a line passing through the point $P$ intersects the circle $x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y + 3 = 0$ at the points $R$ and $S$, then the maximum value of $(PR + PS)^2$ is :
solution:
The equation of the parabola is $y = x^2 - 6x + 12$, which can be rewritten as $y - 3 = (x - 3)^2$.

The vertex of the parabola is $P(3, 3)$.

The equation of the circle is $x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y + 3 = 0$, which can be rewritten as $(x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = 2$.

The center of the circle is $C(1, 2)$ and its radius is $r = \sqrt{2}$.

The distance between the point $P$ and the center $C$ is:

$PC = \sqrt{(3 - 1)^2 + (3 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5}$

Since $PC = \sqrt{5} > r = \sqrt{2}$, the point $P$ lies outside the circle.

Let the line through $P$ intersect the circle at $R$ and $S$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of the chord $RS$. The perpendicular distance from the center $C$ to the chord is $CM$.

Using the right-angled triangle $\triangle PCM$, the distance from $P$ to the midpoint $M$ is $PM = \sqrt{PC^2 - CM^2}$.

The lengths of the segments are $PR = PM - RM$ and $PS = PM + RM$.

Therefore, the sum of the distances is:

$PR + PS = (PM - RM) + (PM + RM) = 2PM = 2\sqrt{PC^2 - CM^2}$

To maximize $(PR + PS)^2$, we need to maximize $PC^2 - CM^2$. This occurs when $CM$ is minimized, which means $CM = 0$. This happens when the line passes through the center $C$ of the circle.

Substituting $CM = 0$, we get the maximum sum:

$PR + PS = 2PC = 2\sqrt{5}$

Thus, the maximum value of $(PR + PS)^2$ is:

$(2\sqrt{5})^2 = 4 \times 5 = 20$

Answer: $20$

q: If one end of a focal chord <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi></math> is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the equation of the tangent to it at <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
question: If one end of a focal chord <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi></math> is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then the equation of the tangent to it at <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
solution:
Let 12,-2 is 2t2,4t⇒t=-12

Parameter of other end of focal chord is t=2

⇒ Point is 8,8

⇒ Equation of tangent is 8y-4x+8=0

⇒2y-x=8

q: Let $A B C D$ be a trapezium whose vertices lie on the parabola $y^2=4 x$. Let the sides $A D$ and $B C$ of the trapezium be parallel to y -axis. If the diagonal AC is of length $\frac{25}{4}$ and it passes through the point $(1,0)$, then the area of $A B C D$ is
question: Let $A B C D$ be a trapezium whose vertices lie on the parabola $y^2=4 x$. Let the sides $A D$ and $B C$ of the trapezium be parallel to y -axis. If the diagonal AC is of length $\frac{25}{4}$ and it passes through the point $(1,0)$, then the area of $A B C D$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}\left(\mathrm{at}_1^2, 2 \mathrm{at}\right) \& \mathrm{C}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{t}_1^2},-\frac{2 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{t}_1}\right) \\ & \text { Length } \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}_1}\right)^2=\frac{25}{4}, \mathrm{t}_1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}_1}= \pm \frac{5}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_1=2 \text { or } \frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{~A}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 1\right), \mathrm{D}\left(\frac{1}{4},-1\right), \mathrm{B}(4,4), \mathrm{C}(4,-4) \end{aligned}$ So, area of trapezium $=\frac{1}{2}(8+2)\left(4-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{75}{4}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A variable circle passes through the fixed point $A(p, q)$ and touches $x$-axis. The locus of the other end of the diameter through $\mathrm{A}$ is
question: A variable circle passes through the fixed point $A(p, q)$ and touches $x$-axis. The locus of the other end of the diameter through $\mathrm{A}$ is
solution:
Let the other end of diameter is $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ then equation of circle is
$$
(x-h)(x-p)+(y-k)(y-q)=0
$$
Put $y=0$, since $x$-axis touches the circle
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow x^2-(h+p) x+(h p+k q)=0 \Rightarrow(h+p)^2=4(h p+k q) \quad(D=0) \\
& \Rightarrow(x-p)^2=4 q y .
\end{aligned}
$$

If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is

If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
solution:
General point on x2=8y is Q4t,2t2

Let Ph,k divide OQ in ratio 1:3

h,k=14t+301+3,12t2+301+3

h,k=t,2t24

h=t & k=t22

k=h22

⇒  x2=2y  is the required locus.

q: If the tangent at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></math> touch the circle <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is:
question: If the tangent at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></math> touch the circle <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is:
solution:
Differentiating x2=y-6 with respect to x,

2x=dydx

dydx1,7=2×1=2

Hence, equation of tangent is y-7=2(x-1)

⇒y=2x+5

Distance from centre =

-6-2-8-512+22

⇒55=5

Radius of circle = 82+62-c

⇒100-c= 5

⇒c=95

q:
Statement-1: The line $x-2 y=2$ meets the parabola, $y^2+2 x=0$ only at the point $(-2,-2)$.
Statement-2: The line $y=m x-\frac{1}{2 m}(m \neq 0)$ is tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-2 x$ at the point $\left(-\frac{1}{2 m^2},-\frac{1}{m}\right)$
question:
Statement-1: The line $x-2 y=2$ meets the parabola, $y^2+2 x=0$ only at the point $(-2,-2)$.
Statement-2: The line $y=m x-\frac{1}{2 m}(m \neq 0)$ is tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-2 x$ at the point $\left(-\frac{1}{2 m^2},-\frac{1}{m}\right)$
options: Statement-1 is true; Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is a correct explanation for statement-1.
Statement-1 is false; Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 a true; Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not a correct explanation for statement-1.
solution:
Both statements are true and statement-2 is the correct explanation of statement-1
$\therefore \quad$ The straight line $y=m x+\frac{a}{m}$ is always a tangent to the parabola $y^2=4 a x$ for any value of $m$.
The co-ordinates of point of contact
$$
\left(\frac{a}{m^2}, \frac{2 a}{m}\right)
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The equation of a common tangent to the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
question: The equation of a common tangent to the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is
solution:
Let y=mx+c be the tangent to y=x2, then

x2-mx-c=0

For tangency, discriminant D=0

⇒m2=-4c     …1

Now let y=mx+c be also tangent to y=-x-22, then

x-22+mx+c=0

For D=0, we get 

m2-8m=4c      …2

From1  and 2, we get

m=0, c=0 or m=4, c=-4

So, there will be two common tangents

y=0 or y=4x-4

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $r$ be the radius of the circle, which touches x -axis at point $(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{a} \lt 0$ and the parabola $\mathrm{y}^2=9 \mathrm{x}$ at the point $(4,6)$. Then $r$ is equal to ________
question: Let $r$ be the radius of the circle, which touches x -axis at point $(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{a} \lt 0$ and the parabola $\mathrm{y}^2=9 \mathrm{x}$ at the point $(4,6)$. Then $r$ is equal to ________
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& (x-a)^2+(y-r)^2=r^2 \\ & (4-a)^2+(6-r)^2=r^2 \\ & 16+a^2-8 a+36+r^2-12 r=r^2 \\ & a^2-8 a-12 r+52=0
\end{aligned}$
Tangent to parabola at $(4,6)$ is
$6.4=9 .\left(\frac{x+4}{2}\right) \text { i.e. } 3 x-4 y+12=0$
This is also tangent to the circle
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad C P=r \\ & \frac{3 a-4 r+12}{5}= \pm r
\end{aligned}$
$3 \mathrm{a}+12=4 \mathrm{r} \pm 5 \mathrm{r}\left\{\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{ar} \\ -\mathrm{r}
\end{array}\right......(1)$
equation of circle is
$(x-a)^2+(y-r)^2=r^2$
satsty $\mathrm{P}(4,6) \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2-8 \mathrm{a}-12 r+52=0 \ldots \ldots$ (2)
From equation (1)
If $\mathrm{a}+4=3 \mathrm{r}$ then $\mathrm{a}=+6$ (rejected)
If $3 a+12=-r$ then $a=-14$ and $r=30$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $L_1, L_2$ be the lines passing through the point $P(0,1)$ and touching the parabola $9 x^2+12 x+18 y-14=0$. Let $Q$ and $R$ be the points on the lines $L_1$ and $L_2$ such that the $\triangle P Q R$ is an isosceles triangle with base $Q R$. If the slopes of the lines $Q R$ are $m_1$ and $m_2$, then $16\left(m_1^2+m_2^2\right)$ is equal to _______
question: Let $L_1, L_2$ be the lines passing through the point $P(0,1)$ and touching the parabola $9 x^2+12 x+18 y-14=0$. Let $Q$ and $R$ be the points on the lines $L_1$ and $L_2$ such that the $\triangle P Q R$ is an isosceles triangle with base $Q R$. If the slopes of the lines $Q R$ are $m_1$ and $m_2$, then $16\left(m_1^2+m_2^2\right)$ is equal to _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 9 x^2+12 x+4=-18(y-1) \\ & (3 x+2)^2=-18(y-1) \\ & \left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2=-2(y-1)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & (0,1) \\ & y=m x+1 \\ & \left(x+\frac{2}{3}\right)^2=-2(y-1) \\ & (3 x+2)^2=-18 m x \\ & 9 x^2+(12+18 m) x+4=0 \\ & 4(6+9 m)^2=4(36) \\ & 6+9 m=6,-6 \\ & m=0, \frac{-4}{3}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \tan \theta=-\frac{4}{3} \\ & \frac{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}=\frac{-4}{3} \\ & \left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}-2\right)\left(2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}+1\right)=0 \\ & \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=2, \frac{-1}{2} \\ & \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{QR}}=\tan \left(90+\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & =-\cot \frac{\theta}{2} \\ & \mathrm{~m}_1=\frac{-1}{2} \quad \mathrm{~m}_2=\frac{-1}{-1 / 2}=2 \\ & 16\left(\mathrm{~m}_1^2+\mathrm{m}_2^2\right)=16\left(\frac{1}{4}+4\right) \\ & =4+64=68\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $a \neq 0$ and the line $2 b x+3 c y+4 d=0$ passes through the points of intersection of the parabolas $y^2=4 a x$ and $x^2=4 a y$, then
question: If $a \neq 0$ and the line $2 b x+3 c y+4 d=0$ passes through the points of intersection of the parabolas $y^2=4 a x$ and $x^2=4 a y$, then
solution: Points of intersection of given parabolas are $(0,0)$ and $(4 a, 4 a)$ $\Rightarrow$ equation of line passing through these points is $y=x$ On comparing this line with the given line $2 b x+3 c y+4 d=0$, we get $d=0$ and $2 b+3 c=0 \Rightarrow(2 b+3 c)^2+d^2=0$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the length of the focal chord PQ of the parabola $y^2=12 x$ be 15 units. If the distance of $\mathrm{PQ}$ from the origin is $\mathrm{p}$, then $10 \mathrm{p}^2$ is equal to _______
question: Let the length of the focal chord PQ of the parabola $y^2=12 x$ be 15 units. If the distance of $\mathrm{PQ}$ from the origin is $\mathrm{p}$, then $10 \mathrm{p}^2$ is equal to _______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { length of focal chord }=4 a \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta=15 \\ & 12 \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta=15 \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{4}{5} \\ & \tan ^2 \theta=4 \\ & \tan \theta=2 \\ & \text { equation } \frac{y-0}{x-3}=2 \\ & y=2 x-6 \\ & 2 x-y-6=0 \\ & P=\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \\ & 10 p^2=10 \cdot \frac{36}{5}=72\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the equation of the parabola with vertex $\mathrm{V}\left(\frac{3}{2}, 3\right)$ and the directrix $x+2 y=0$ is $\alpha x^2+\beta y^2-\gamma x y-30 x-60 y+225=0$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to :
question: If the equation of the parabola with vertex $\mathrm{V}\left(\frac{3}{2}, 3\right)$ and the directrix $x+2 y=0$ is $\alpha x^2+\beta y^2-\gamma x y-30 x-60 y+225=0$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Equation of parabola is } P S=(1) P M \\ & \Rightarrow P S^2=P M^2 \\ & (x-3)^2+(y-6)^2=\left(\frac{x+2 y}{\sqrt{5}}\right)^2 \\ & 5 x^2-30 x+5 y^2-60 y+225=x^2+4 y^2+4 x y \\ & 4 x^2+y^2-4 x y-30 x-60 y+225=0 \end{aligned}$ We get : $\alpha=4, \beta=1, \gamma=4$ $\therefore \alpha+\beta+\gamma=9$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. If the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the center of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is minimum, then the equation of the tangent to the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. If the distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> from the center of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is minimum, then the equation of the tangent to the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
solution:
Let P2t,t2.

Equation normal at P to x2=4y be

y-t2=-1tx-2t.

It passes a normal through -3,0.

⇒0-t2=-1t-3-2t

⇒t3+2t+3=0

⇒t+1t2-t+3=0

⇒t=-1

So, point P is -2,1.

Equation of a tangent to x2=4y at -2,1.

⇒x-2=2y+1

∴x+y+1=0.

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two distinct points on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, with parameters <math><mi>t</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math>, respectively. If the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the minimum value of <math><msubsup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msubsup></math> , is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two distinct points on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, with parameters <math><mi>t</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math>, respectively. If the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the minimum value of <math><msubsup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msubsup></math> , is
solution:
Given Pt2, 2t and Qt12, 2t1, are parametric points on parabola
y2=4x
Slope of normal at P=-t

Since normal passes through Q, 

⇒  slope of normal at P = slope of PQ

⇒ -t=2t1-tt12-t2

⇒ -t=2t1+t

⇒t1=-t-2t

⇒t12=t2+4t2+4   ...1

t2+4t2≥2t2.4t2≥4,

Using AM≥GM

∴  t12≥4+4   from 1

⇒ t12≥8

∴   Minimum value=8

q:
Statement $1$: An equation of a common tangent to the parabola $y^2=16 \sqrt{3} x$ and the ellipse $2 x^2+y^2=4$ is $y=2 x+2 \sqrt{3}$.
Statement $2$: If the line $y=m x+\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{m},(m \neq 0)$ is a common tangent to the parabola $y^2=16 \sqrt{3} x$ and the ellipse $2 x^2+y^2=4$, then $m$ satisfies $m^4+2 m^2=24$.
question:
Statement $1$: An equation of a common tangent to the parabola $y^2=16 \sqrt{3} x$ and the ellipse $2 x^2+y^2=4$ is $y=2 x+2 \sqrt{3}$.
Statement $2$: If the line $y=m x+\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{m},(m \neq 0)$ is a common tangent to the parabola $y^2=16 \sqrt{3} x$ and the ellipse $2 x^2+y^2=4$, then $m$ satisfies $m^4+2 m^2=24$.
options: Statement $1$ is false, statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is true; statement $2$ is a correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is true; statement $2$ is not a correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, statement $2$ is false
solution:
$y^2=16 \sqrt{3} x$
$\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$
$y=m x+\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{m}$ is tangent to parabola
which is tangent to ellipse
$\Rightarrow c^2=a^2 m^2+b^2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{48}{m^2}=2 m^2+4 \quad \Rightarrow m^4+2 m^2=24 \quad \Rightarrow m^2=4$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y^{2}=12 x$ be the parabola with its vertex at O. Let P be a point on the parabola and A be a point on the $x$-axis such that $\angle \mathrm{OPA}=90^{\circ}$. Then the locus of the centroid of such triangles OPA is :
question: Let $y^{2}=12 x$ be the parabola with its vertex at O. Let P be a point on the parabola and A be a point on the $x$-axis such that $\angle \mathrm{OPA}=90^{\circ}$. Then the locus of the centroid of such triangles OPA is :
solution:
Parabola $y^2 = 12x$, $a = 3$. Let $P = (3t^2, 6t)$, $O = (0,0)$, $A = (h, 0)$.
$\angle OPA = 90^\circ \Rightarrow \vec{PO} \cdot \vec{PA} = 0$:
$(-3t^2)(h - 3t^2) + (-6t)(-6t) = 0 \Rightarrow h = 3t^2 + 12$.
Centroid $G = \left(\frac{0 + 3t^2 + h}{3}, \frac{0 + 6t + 0}{3}\right) = (2t^2 + 4, \ 2t)$.
Let $G = (X, Y)$: $t = Y/2$, $X = Y^2/2 + 4$.
Locus: $y^2 - 2x + 8 = 0$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A, B$ and $C$ be three points on the parabola $y^2=6 x$ and let the line segment $A B$ meet the line $L$ through $C$ parallel to the $x$-axis at the point $D$. Let $M$ and $N$ respectively be the feet of the perpendiculars from $A$ and $B$ on $L$. Then $\left(\frac{A M \cdot B N}{C D}\right)^2$ is equal to _________
question: Let $A, B$ and $C$ be three points on the parabola $y^2=6 x$ and let the line segment $A B$ meet the line $L$ through $C$ parallel to the $x$-axis at the point $D$. Let $M$ and $N$ respectively be the feet of the perpendiculars from $A$ and $B$ on $L$. Then $\left(\frac{A M \cdot B N}{C D}\right)^2$ is equal to _________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AD}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1-\mathrm{t}_3\right)}{\mathrm{at}_1^2-\alpha} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{at}_1^2-\alpha=\mathrm{a}\left\{\mathrm{t}_1^2-\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_3+\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_3\right\} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_3+\mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2\right) \\ & \mathrm{AM}=\left|2 \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1-\mathrm{t}_3\right)\right|, \mathrm{BN}=\left|2 \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_3\right)\right|, \\ & \mathrm{CD}=\left|\mathrm{at}_3^2-\alpha\right|\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CD}=\left|\mathrm{at}_3^2-\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_3+\mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2\right)\right| \\ &=\mathrm{a}\left|\mathrm{t}_3^2-\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_2 \mathrm{t}_3+\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2\right| \\ &=\mathrm{a}\left|\mathrm{t}_3\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1\right)-\mathrm{t}_2\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1\right)\right| \\ & \mathrm{CD}=\mathrm{a}\left|\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1\right)\right| \\ &\left(\frac{\mathrm{AM} \cdot \mathrm{BN}}{\mathrm{CD}}\right)^2=\left\{\frac{2 \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_1-\mathrm{t}_3\right) \cdot 2 \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_3\right)}{\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{t}_3-\mathrm{t}_1\right)}\right\}^2 \\ & 16 \mathrm{a}^2=16 \times \frac{9}{4}=36\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The locus of a point which divides the line segment joining the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is:
question: The locus of a point which divides the line segment joining the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and a point on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is:
solution:
Let point P be 2t, t2 and Q be h, k .
h=2t3, k=-2+t23
Hence, locus is 3k+2=3h2s2⇒9x2=12y+8
